text.title: Web Scraping Agentic Workflow Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules every agent-driven scrape pipeline must satisfy. The scrape-run report validator enforces them at end-of-run.

rule.statement: Before tool selection the agent MUST run a curl -I + small GET probe on the canonical URL and classify render mode ∈ {ssr, js, managed}. SSR = listing items present in initial HTML; JS = listing items absent until after JS execution.
rule.rationale: Skipping the probe routinely picks Playwright for SSR pages, multiplying CPU + memory cost by 50x and slowing each page load by 8s.

rule.statement: Tool selection MUST match render mode: ssr → httpx + selectolax; js → Playwright (Chromium); managed → Firecrawl / r.jina.ai. Mismatches are rejected by the validator.
rule.rationale: A 6 KB JSON response with httpx costs ~1 ms; the same page through Playwright costs ~3 s. Managed services save 70% LLM tokens by returning cleaned Markdown.

rule.statement: Extraction MUST use role/text/structural locators (e.g. role="listitem", text="Add to cart", "article > h2") — NOT class-name selectors fabricated by the agent.
rule.rationale: Agents invent class names that "look right" (.product-card) when the actual class is .css-1abc23. Locators by role/text survive class-name churn.

rule.statement: Every extracted row passes Pydantic/Zod validation BEFORE persistence. Invalid rows are written to a separate `invalid/<source>.jsonl` stream with the validation error; rows_valid / rows_seen ratio appears in the run report.
rule.rationale: Silent ingestion of malformed rows contaminates the dataset for weeks. Eager validation surfaces drift at the boundary.

rule.statement: Output writes to `raw/YYYY-MM-DD/<source>.jsonl` (one row per line). The raw HTML snapshot is stored alongside the parsed JSON under `raw/YYYY-MM-DD/<source>.html.gz`.
rule.rationale: Date-partitioned JSONL is the cheapest format that supports re-parse without re-scrape. The HTML snapshot lets the parser be improved without paying the scrape cost again.

rule.statement: Drift score = % of declared schema fields that appeared in ≥80% of yesterday's rows but appeared in <80% of today's rows. If drift > 5%, the run STOPS, a human-review issue is filed, and no downstream stage may consume today's `raw/`.
rule.rationale: Source-side template changes cause silent data loss. A 5% threshold catches structural shifts without false-positives on noisy fields.

rule.statement: robots.txt is fetched and cached PER RUN as the first action. User-Agent identifies the bot with a contact email (e.g. `faion-network/1.0 (+mailto:ruslan@faion.net)`). If robots.txt disallows the URL pattern, the run aborts.
rule.rationale: robots.txt compliance is a legal shield in many jurisdictions; identifying contact UA cuts ban rate dramatically when site owners reach out instead of blocking.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (target URL, schema, storage path, contact UA), execute steps r7 → r1 → r2 → r3 → r4 → r5 → r6 in that order. Emit the scrape-run report at end; do not skip drift even on clean runs.
rule.rationale: Skipping a step under "looks fine" pressure is the principal failure mode for LLM-driven scrapes.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) the source provides an official API — use it, (b) robots.txt disallows AND no legal exception, (c) a one-off scrape with no recurrence — quick-and-dirty is fine, (d) the source returns persistent 429 even at low rates — load `web-scraping-resilience` first.
rule.rationale: Agentic-workflow overhead assumes recurrence + permitted access; below those, the artefact burden is wasted.
